abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a powerful paradigm for open-world robot manipulation, but their practical deployment is often constrained by cost: billion-scale VLM backbones and iterative diffusion/flow-based action heads incur high latency and compute, making real-time control expensive on commodity hardware. We present A1, a fully open-source and transparent VLA framework designed for low-cost, high-throughput inference without sacrificing manipulation success; Our approach leverages pretrained VLMs that provide implicit affordance priors for action generation. We release the full training stack (training code, data/data-processing pipeline, intermediate checkpoints, and evaluation scripts) to enable end-to-end reproducibility. Beyond optimizing the VLM alone, A1 targets the full inference pipeline by introducing a budget-aware adaptive inference scheme that jointly accelerates the backbone and the action head. Specifically, we monitor action consistency across intermediate VLM layers to trigger early termination, and propose Inter-Layer Truncated Flow Matching that warm-starts denoising across layers, enabling accurate actions with substantially fewer effective denoising iterations. Across simulation benchmarks (LIBERO, VLABench) and real robots (Franka, AgiBot), A1 achieves state-of-the-art success rates while significantly reducing inference cost (e.g., up to 72% lower per-episode latency for flow-matching inference and up to 76.6% backbone computation reduction with minor performance degradation). On RoboChallenge, A1 achieves an average success rate of 29.00%, outperforming baselines including pi0(28.33%), X-VLA (21.33%), and RDT-1B (15.00%).

citations.fullCitation: Kevin Black, Noah Brown, Danny Driess, Adnan Esmail, Michael Equi, Chelsea Finn, Niccolo Fusai, Lachy Groom, Karol Hausman, Brian Ichter, et al. π0: A vision-language-action flow model for general robot control. arXiv preprint arXiv:2410.24164, 2024.
citations.justification: This paper introduces π0, a state-of-the-art model that serves as a primary baseline for A1 in performance comparisons. A1's flow-matching action head is also directly based on the methods proposed in π0, making it a crucial point of comparison and a source of key methodological components.
citations.title: π0: A vision-language-action flow model for general robot control

citations.fullCitation: Yang Yue, Yulin Wang, Bingyi Kang, Yizeng Han, Shenzhi Wang, Shiji Song, Jiashi Feng, and Gao Huang. Deer-vla: Dynamic inference of multimodal large language models for efficient robot execution. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 37:56619–56643, 2024.
citations.justification: This work is cited as the direct inspiration for A1's main technical contribution: the adaptive early-exit inference mechanism for accelerating computation. The A1 paper explicitly builds upon the ideas in DeeR-VLA to create its budget-aware acceleration scheme, making this a foundational citation for its core novelty.
citations.title: Deer-vla: Dynamic inference of multimodal large language models for efficient robot execution

citations.fullCitation: Matt Deitke, Christopher Clark, Sangho Lee, Rohun Tripathi, Yue Yang, Jae Sung Park, Mohammadreza Salehi, Niklas Muennighoff, Kyle Lo, Luca Soldaini, Jiasen Lu, Taira Anderson, Erin Bransom, Kiana Ehsani, Huong Ngo, YenSung Chen, Ajay Patel, Mark Yatskar, Chris Callison-Burch, Andrew Head, Rose Hendrix, Favyen Bastani, Eli VanderBilt, Nathan Lambert, Yvonne Chou, Arnavi Chheda, Jenna Sparks, Sam Skjonsberg, Michael Schmitz, Aaron Sarnat, Byron Bischoff, Pete Walsh, Chris Newell, Piper Wolters, Tanmay Gupta, Kuo-Hao Zeng, Jon Borchardt, Dirk Groeneveld, Crystal Nam, Sophie Lebrecht, Caitlin Wittlif, Carissa Schoenick, Oscar Michel, Ranjay Krishna, Luca Weihs, Noah A. Smith, Hannaneh Hajishirzi, Ross Girshick, Ali Farhadi, and Aniruddha Kembhavi. Molmo and pixmo: Open weights and open data for state-of-the-art vision-language models, 2024. https://arxiv.org/abs/2409.17146.
citations.justification: A1's architecture is fundamentally built upon the Molmo Vision-Language Model as its backbone. This citation is critical as Molmo provides the pre-trained visual-semantic understanding and implicit affordance priors that A1 leverages for its powerful manipulation capabilities.
citations.title: Molmo and pixmo: Open weights and open data for state-of-the-art vision-language models

citations.fullCitation: Alexander Khazatsky, Karl Pertsch, Suraj Nair, Ashwin Balakrishna, Sudeep Dasari, Siddharth Karamcheti, Soroush Nasiriany, Mohan Kumar Srirama, Lawrence Yunliang Chen, Kirsty Ellis, et al. Droid: A large-scale in-the-wild robot manipulation dataset. arXiv preprint arXiv:2403.12945, 2024.
citations.justification: The A1 paper attributes its robust generalization to pre-training on diverse, large-scale, open-source robotic datasets. DROID is a prominent example of the datasets used, representing the critical data foundation that enables A1's performance across various robots, tasks, and environments.
citations.title: Droid: A large-scale in-the-wild robot manipulation dataset

intermediateReport:
The following report provides a detailed analysis of the research paper "A1: A Fully Transparent Open-Source, Adaptive and Efficient Truncated Vision-Language-Action Model."

---

### **Research Report: A1: A Fully Transparent Open-Source, Adaptive and Efficient Truncated Vision-Language-Action Model**

### **1. Authors and Institution(s)**

The research was conducted by a collaborative team of authors primarily affiliated with:
*   **SYSU** (Sun Yat-sen University)
*   **MBZUAI** (Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence)
*   **Spatialtemporal AI**

Key contributors include Kaidong Zhang, Jian Zhang, and Rongtao Xu, who are noted as having equal contributions, with Rongtao Xu serving as Project Lead and Xiaodan Liang as the corresponding author.

### **2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape**

The field of robotic manipulation has seen the emergence of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models as a paradigm for enabling robots to operate in open-world scenarios. These models typically integrate large-scale Vision-Language Model (VLM) backbones for understanding complex visual scenes and linguistic instructions, coupled with action heads (often based on diffusion or flow matching) for generating continuous motor commands. This architecture allows VLAs to leverage strong semantic priors from pre-trained VLMs and expressive generative decoders, resulting in generalized manipulation capabilities across various objects, instructions, and robot morphologies.

However, the practical deployment of these advanced VLA models is frequently hindered by their computational demands. State-of-the-art VLAs often rely on multi-billion-parameter VLMs and require numerous iterative denoising steps (typically 10-20) from their diffusion or flow-matching action heads. While existing research has explored methods to reduce VLM latency through techniques such as quantization, sparsity, or early-exit mechanisms, the action head often remains an unaddressed bottleneck, thereby increasing overall inference latency and computational cost. This necessitates expensive hardware and substantial energy budgets, limiting real-time control and widespread adoption on commodity hardware.

This research aims to address these efficiency limitations by proposing a framework that jointly optimizes both the VLM backbone and the action head during inference. It contributes to the growing body of work focused on developing efficient VLA models, distinguishing itself by introducing an adaptive inference scheme and an inter-layer warm-starting technique for flow-matching action heads, alongside a commitment to full transparency and open-source release of the entire training and inference stack.

### **3. Key Objectives and Motivation**

The primary objective of this research is to develop **A1**, a fully open-source and transparent VLA framework designed for low-cost, high-throughput inference without compromising manipulation success rates. The authors aim to overcome the practical constraints imposed by the high computational cost and latency of existing VLA models, which impede their real-time deployment on standard hardware.

The motivation for A1 is rooted in three empirical observations regarding the operational characteristics of current VLA models:

1.  **Trajectory Convergence:** The authors observed that flow-matching trajectories often converge to the correct action mode within a limited number of denoising steps (eewer than three), with subsequent iterations primarily contributing to minor precision refinements that exhibit diminishing returns. This suggests that extensive iterative denoising might be computationally redundant in many scenarios.
2.  **Action Redundancy:** Across consecutive control steps, many robot actions evolve smoothly and may only require coarse updates rather than entirely new computations. This implies that full re-evaluation of the VLA at every timestep could be inefficient.
3.  **Layer-wise Coupling:** Intermediate hidden states within the VLM backbone are observed to already encode sufficient spatial and visual features to adequately seed downstream action prediction processes, such as the flow-matching vector field. This indicates that a full forward pass through all layers of the VLM backbone might not always be necessary for accurate action generation.

These observations collectively suggest a core principle for achieving high-efficiency VLA inference: computational resources should be expended judiciously, only when they are likely to result in a material change in the predicted action. Based on this principle, A1 proposes a budget-aware adaptive inference mechanism that seeks to reduce redundant computation in both the VLM backbone and the iterative action head.

Secondary objectives include:
*   Leveraging pre-trained VLMs, specifically Molmo, to provide implicit affordance priors for action generation, thereby enhancing the semantic richness of the representations used for action prediction.
*   Achieving robust generalization across diverse robot embodiments and tasks by pre-training A1 on a comprehensive collection of open-source and in-house robotic datasets.
*   Promoting transparency and reproducibility within the robotics research community by releasing the complete training stack, including code, data processing pipelines, intermediate checkpoints, and evaluation scripts.

### **4. Methodology and Approach**

The methodology adopted for A1 focuses on developing an efficient VLA architecture and an adaptive inference mechanism, supported by a structured training pipeline.

**A1 Architecture:**
The A1 architecture integrates a Vision-Language Model (VLM) backbone with a specialized action head.
*   **VLM Backbone:** The VLM's weights are initialized from Molmo (a 7B parameter model), which is reported to provide robust visual-semantic understanding and implicit affordance priors derived from large-scale multimodal pre-training. The VLM processes multimodal observations (images from multiple cameras, robot proprioceptive state, and language instructions) into a latent representation.
*   **Action Head:** Two instantiations of the action head are provided:
    *   **Flow Matching (A1-FM):** This head is based on a Qwen3 model (approximately 400M parameters). It is trained using a conditional flow matching loss, where the network learns to predict a denoising vector field. During inference, actions are generated by integrating this learned vector field from random noise.
    *   **MLP Head (A1-MLP):** This simpler head uses an MLP to regress continuous actions from the hidden states of dedicated action tokens, supervised by an L1 loss. It is intended for faster fitting and noise suppression.

**Adaptive Inference Acceleration:**
A core contribution of A1 is its budget-aware adaptive inference mechanism, which jointly accelerates the VLM backbone and the action head.
*   **Multi-exit Training:** During training, the model is designed to be executable at various depths. Specifically, a random VLM layer index `i` is sampled, and the action head’s loss is supervised using the hidden state from that layer. This prepares the VLM to produce meaningful action predictions at intermediate layers. Alternatively, action losses can be supervised from all layers to achieve more stable training.
*   **Early-Termination Inference via Action-Consistency Thresholding:**
    *   At inference time, the VLM processes input layer-by-layer. After each eligible layer `i`, the action head generates an action chunk `A^(i)`.
    *   An action-consistency test is performed: `∆_i = d(A^(i), A^(i-1)) < η_i`, where `d(·,·)` is a discrepancy metric (e.g., L2 distance, cosine similarity, or mean absolute deviation) and `η_i` is a layer-specific threshold.
    *   If the discrepancy falls below the threshold, indicating action stabilization, the forward pass is terminated early, and `A^(i)` is taken as the final action.
    *   The thresholds `η_i` are calibrated offline from training data. For a given compute budget, a target early-exit probability distribution (e.g., exponential distribution emphasizing earlier exits) is used to determine quantiles of layer-wise action discrepancies, ensuring disjoint assignment across exits.
*   **Inter-Layer Truncated Flow Matching (for A1-FM):**
    *   This technique addresses the computational overhead of iterative denoising in the FM action head during early-exit inference.
    *   Instead of performing a full `δ` (e.g., 10 or 20) denoising steps at each potential exit layer, `δ` is reduced to a small value (e.g., 2 steps).
    *   Crucially, when transitioning from layer `i` to `i+1`, the denoising process for layer `i+1` is warm-started with the output action `A^(i)_1` from layer `i`, rather than re-starting from random noise. This propagation of denoising across layers significantly reduces the effective number of denoising iterations and computational cost, while maintaining accuracy.

**Training Recipe:**
A two-stage training pipeline is employed:
1.  **Pre-Training:**
    *   **Data Composition:** A large-scale, robot-trajectory-only corpus is built from:
        *   **Open-source Robotic Datasets:** DROID, AgiBot, RoboCOIN, RoboMind, GM-100, and RoboChallenge, offering diverse robot morphologies and task families.
        *   **Collected Robotic Dataset:** 15,951 in-house trajectories on ARX, Franka, UR5, and Agibot platforms, collected to align with target deployment conditions and reduce the sim-to-real gap.
    *   **Unified Representation and Quality Control:** All datasets are converted into a consistent episodic format (`(o_t, s_t, ℓ, a_t)`). Lightweight filtering removes corrupted or redundant episodes, and balanced sampling is applied across sources.
2.  **Fine-Tuning:** The pre-trained model is fine-tuned on smaller, high-quality demonstration datasets for specific robot embodiments or downstream tasks.
*   **Data Processing and Augmentation:** Visual inputs undergo sharpening and random erasing. Proprioceptive states are augmented with random masking (state zero-out). State normalization is intentionally avoided across datasets to preserve physical dynamics. Static frames and low-velocity segments are filtered.
*   **Data Sampling Strategy:** A hierarchical balanced sampling strategy is used (dataset-level then embodiment-level) to ensure balanced exposure to diverse hardware and task distributions.
*   **Optimization and Learning Rate Scheduling:** The Vision Transformer (ViT) backbone is frozen during training. VLM components and the action head are optimized with different learning rates (5 × 10⁻⁶ and 5 × 10⁻⁵, respectively) using AdamW. A warm-up strategy is followed by cosine annealing.

### **5. Main Findings and Results**

The experimental evaluation of A1 was conducted across various simulation and real-world environments, demonstrating its performance, efficiency, and generalization capabilities.

**Model Computational Cost Analysis:**
The analysis indicates that a Molmo-7B VLM has a total inference cost of 11,074.39 GFLOPs (with a sequence length of 352). A1-FM's normal inference requires 11.130 TFLOPs. For early-exit inference, if A1-FM runs to the final layer with δ=10 denoising steps, the cost is 11.160 TFLOPs and 4.44s. However, reducing δ to 2 steps decreases inference time to 0.73s. This highlights that while the flow-matching model itself has a modest computational cost, the iterative denoising steps significantly increase inference time, underscoring the necessity of Inter-Layer Truncated Flow Matching.

**Simulation Benchmark Results:**
*   **LIBERO:** A1 achieved an average success rate of 96.6%, with a particularly high success rate of 99.8% on the OBJECT task. This performance surpasses several baselines, including Octo (75.1%), OpenVLA (76.5%), π0 (94.2%), and π0.5 (96.9%).
*   **VLABench:** A1 obtained an average success rate of 53.5%, outperforming π0.5 by 4%. Failures were primarily attributed to objects falling rather than task misidentification, suggesting robust task understanding.

**Real-World Experiment Results:**
*   **Real-world Manipulation (Franka, AgiBot, WuJie-Arm, Dobot-Arm):** Across seven diverse manipulation tasks on four robotic platforms, A1 achieved an average success rate of 56.7%. This outcome represents a significant improvement over π0.5 (47.5%) and π0 (40.8%). A1 demonstrated superior performance in fine manipulation (e.g., AgiBot’s "pick glue" task with 80% success) and long-horizon tasks (e.g., "clean table" with 20% success). Notably, with only 50 samples for the "fruits (small)" task, A1 achieved 50% success, surpassing baselines by 20-40%. Qualitative results indicated more accurate action execution compared to baselines.
*   **RoboChallenge Results (Table30 benchmark):** A1 achieved an average success rate of 29.00%, ranking sixth overall and outperforming comparable open-source baselines such as π0 (28.33%), X-VLA (21.33%), and RDT-1B (15.00%). Specific strong performances include "Open Drawer" (100%), "Put Cup on Coaster" (90%), and "Stack Bowls" (80%), demonstrating practical viability in complex real-robot tasks.

**Ablation Study:**
*   **Effectiveness of Early-Termination Inference (A1-MLP):** Using the A1-MLP model on the LIBERO benchmark, different exit criteria `c` (based on an exponential distribution) were tested.
    *   With `c=1.0`, A1-MLP achieved 96.6% average success, reducing layer computation by 15.6% compared to full inference.
    *   As `c` decreased, computation reduction increased significantly (up to 76.6% with `c=0.1`), with a minor degradation in success rate (e.g., 92.3% with 76.6% computation reduction).
    *   In some cases (e.g., LIBERO-Spatial with `c=0.7`), less computation resulted in slightly higher performance, suggesting the model's ability to adaptively select effective features. Visualizations showed earlier exits for simple actions and deeper layer evaluations for complex ones.
*   **Effectiveness of Inter-Layer Truncated Flow Matching (A1-FM):**
    *   Standard early-exit inference with A1-FM (c=1.0, δ=10) reduced computation but increased inference time due to iterative denoising at each layer.
    *   By contrast, Inter-Layer Truncated Flow Matching (c=1.0, δ=2, warm-started) reduced per-episode inference time from 37.8s to 10.5s (a 72.3% reduction) while maintaining a 96.4% success rate.
    *   Warm-start initialization also improved the success rate from 95.4% to 96.4%.

**Generalization Experiments (LIBERO-Plus):**
When evaluated zero-shot on the LIBERO-Plus benchmark, A1-FM (trained on standard LIBERO) achieved a robust success rate of 75.3%. This performance exceeded OpenVLA-OFT (69.6%), π0 (53.6%), and π0-FAST (61.6%), even under significant distribution shifts in object layouts, language instructions, textures, and lighting.

### **6. Significance and Potential Impact**

The A1 framework represents a notable advancement in the development of Vision-Language-Action models for robotic manipulation. Its significance and potential impact stem from several key aspects:

1.  **Enhanced Practical Deployability:** By effectively addressing the computational cost and latency bottlenecks inherent in VLA models, A1 enables real-time robotic control on more accessible and commodity hardware. This substantially lowers the barrier to entry for deploying advanced VLA policies in various real-world applications, moving beyond research labs.
2.  **State-of-the-Art Performance with Efficiency:** A1 demonstrates that significant efficiency gains do not necessitate a compromise in manipulation performance. It achieves competitive or superior success rates across a range of simulation benchmarks (LIBERO, VLABench) and real-world tasks on diverse robotic platforms (Franka, AgiBot, WuJie-Arm, Dobot-Arm, RoboChallenge). This establishes a precedent for high-performance, efficient VLA design.
3.  **Transparency and Reproducibility:** The commitment to open-sourcing the entire training stack (code, data processing, intermediate checkpoints, evaluation scripts) is a substantial contribution to the research community. This full transparency fosters reproducibility, allows for independent auditing of results, and accelerates future research by providing a robust, modifiable baseline for subsequent developments in efficient VLA models.
4.  **Robust Generalization Capabilities:** The model’s pre-training on a diverse corpus of open-source and collected robotic data, combined with its strong zero-shot transfer performance on challenging benchmarks like LIBERO-Plus, indicates robust generalization across varied embodiments, tasks, and environmental conditions. This makes A1 more adaptable to new, unseen scenarios.
5.  **Methodological Innovation:** The introduction of a budget-aware adaptive inference scheme, particularly the Inter-Layer Truncated Flow Matching with warm-start denoising, provides a novel approach to jointly optimizing the computational overhead of both the VLM backbone and iterative action heads. This methodological contribution can inform future research into efficient deep learning architectures for real-time control.
6.  **Future Research Directions:** The work identifies limitations and outlines future research avenues, such as exploring unsupervised affordance learning, integrating reinforcement learning for improved robustness against cumulative errors, and addressing issues of synchronization and network latency for smoother robot manipulation. The dual-arm mobile control platform under development also points towards A1's potential in more complex, mobile manipulation scenarios.

In summary, A1 contributes to making sophisticated robotic manipulation more accessible and practical, thereby accelerating the transition of VLA models from theoretical constructs to deployable, real-world robotic intelligence.
overview:
## Efficient Robotic Intelligence via Adaptive Inference

Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a powerful framework for enabling robots to understand complex human instructions and execute motor commands in diverse, open-world environments. By integrating large-scale Vision-Language Models (VLMs) with expressive action decoders—often based on diffusion or flow-matching techniques—these systems can generalize across different objects and robot morphologies. However, the high computational cost of these multi-billion-parameter models creates a significant bottleneck for real-time control. Most state-of-the-art VLAs require substantial GPU resources and suffer from high inference latency, limiting their use on standard commodity hardware.

![A1 Framework Overview](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05672v2/x1.png)
*Figure 1: The A1 framework integrates a VLM backbone with adaptive inference and an efficient action head to achieve high-throughput robotic control.*

The A1 framework addresses these efficiency challenges by introducing an adaptive, budget-aware inference mechanism. The core philosophy behind A1 is that not every robotic task requires the full computational depth of a large model. Many actions are redundant across consecutive timesteps, and the trajectories often converge to the correct mode within just a few iterations. By allowing the model to "exit" early when an action prediction stabilizes, A1 significantly reduces the total floating-point operations (FLOPs) required for execution without sacrificing success rates.

## Architecture and Core Principles

A1 is built upon a vision-language backbone and a specialized action head designed for continuous control. The model is initialized with weights from Molmo-7B, a vision-language model that provides a strong semantic understanding of visual scenes. This backbone processes multimodal inputs—including images from multiple camera views, the robot's proprioceptive state (its current joint positions or end-effector pose), and high-level linguistic instructions.

The framework offers two types of action heads:
1.  **A1-FM (Flow Matching):** This head is based on a Qwen3 model (400M parameters) and utilizes conditional flow matching. It learns to predict a vector field that "pushes" random noise toward a valid action sequence.
2.  **A1-MLP:** A simpler, faster head that uses a multi-layer perceptron to regress actions directly from the VLM's hidden states.

The design of A1 is motivated by the observation that intermediate layers of a VLM often already contain enough information to suggest a correct action. For instance, identifying a "red cup" might only require a few layers of processing, whereas determining the exact millimeter-scale grasping point might require deeper evaluation. A1 exploits this by training the model to be functional at various depths, a process known as multi-exit training.

## Budget-Aware Adaptive Inference

A significant contribution of A1 is its ability to dynamically adjust its computational load based on the complexity of the current task. This is achieved through a combination of multi-exit training and action-consistency thresholding.

### Multi-exit Training and Early Termination

During the training phase, the model is supervised not just at its final layer, but at multiple intermediate layers. A random layer index $i$ is sampled, and the action head is trained using the hidden state from that layer. This ensures that every layer $i$ is capable of producing a meaningful action chunk $A^{(i)}$.

At inference time, A1 processes the input through the VLM backbone layer-by-layer. After each eligible layer, it generates a candidate action and performs a consistency test to decide whether to continue processing or exit early:

$$\Delta_i = d(A^{(i)}, A^{(i-1)}) < \eta_i$$

In this equation, $d(\cdot, \cdot)$ represents a discrepancy metric (such as L2 distance or cosine similarity) between the action predicted at the current layer $A^{(i)}$ and the action predicted at the previous layer $A^{(i-1)}$. If the difference $\Delta_i$ is smaller than a pre-calibrated, layer-specific threshold $\eta_i$, the model concludes that further processing will yield diminishing returns and terminates the forward pass early. This allows the system to save power and time on simple or repetitive actions.

![Adaptive Inference Mechanism](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05672v2/x2.png)
*Figure 2: The multi-exit training and adaptive inference pipeline of A1, illustrating how the model determines the optimal layer for action output.*

### Inter-Layer Truncated Flow Matching

While early-exit mechanisms accelerate the VLM backbone, the action head can still be a bottleneck, especially when using iterative methods like flow matching. Standard flow matching might require 10 to 20 denoising steps $\delta$ at every exit layer to produce a high-quality action.

A1 introduces "Inter-Layer Truncated Flow Matching" to solve this. Instead of re-starting the denoising process from random noise at every layer, A1 reduces the number of denoising steps (for example, to $\delta = 2$) and "warm-starts" the process. When moving from layer $i$ to layer $i+1$, the action head uses the result $A^{(i)}_1$ from the previous layer as the starting point for the next layer's refinement. This propagation of information across layers ensures that the denoising trajectory remains continuous and efficient, drastically reducing total inference time.

## Data and Training Strategy

To achieve robust generalization, A1 was trained on a massive, unified dataset. This corpus combines several major open-source robotic datasets (such as DROID and AgiBot) with over 15,000 in-house trajectories collected across various platforms, including Franka, UR5, and ARX arms.

### Unified Representation

All training data is converted into a consistent format represented as $(o_t, s_t, \ell, a_t)$, where:
- $o_t$ is the visual observation.
- $s_t$ is the robot state.
- $\ell$ is the language instruction.
- $a_t$ is the target action.

The training process involves two stages. First, a large-scale pre-training phase on the general robot trajectory corpus helps the model learn universal manipulation priors. Second, a fine-tuning phase on high-quality demonstration data tailors the model to specific robot embodiments or tasks. The optimization uses different learning rates for the VLM (around $5 \times 10^{-6}$) and the action head (around $5 \times 10^{-5}$) to balance the preservation of semantic knowledge with the learning of precise motor control.

## Performance and Evaluation

A1 has been rigorously tested in both simulated benchmarks and real-world environments. The results demonstrate that the model achieves state-of-the-art performance while maintaining high efficiency.

### Simulation Benchmarks

In the LIBERO benchmark, which tests a wide range of manipulation tasks, A1 achieved an average success rate of 96.6%, outperforming popular baselines like OpenVLA and Octo. On the VLABench, A1 obtained a 53.5% success rate, showing a 4% improvement over comparable models. 

![LIBERO Simulation Results](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05672v2/x4.png)
*Figure 3: Qualitative comparison of A1 and baseline models on simulation tasks, highlighting A1's higher success rates in multi-step manipulation.*

### Real-World Deployment

The real-world evaluation spanned multiple robotic platforms and diverse tasks, from picking up small objects to long-horizon cleaning tasks. A1 achieved an average real-world success rate of 56.7%, significantly higher than the 47.5% achieved by π0.5. One of the most notable findings was A1's efficiency: using Inter-Layer Truncated Flow Matching, the researchers reduced inference time by 72.3% (from 37.8 seconds down to 10.5 seconds per episode) with virtually no loss in success rate (96.4% vs 96.6%).

![Real-World Task Execution](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05672v2/x3.png)
*Figure 4: A1 successfully performing diverse manipulation tasks across different robot arms in real-world settings.*

### Generalization

A1 also shows strong zero-shot generalization capabilities. When tested on the LIBERO-Plus benchmark—which introduces significant shifts in lighting, object textures, and layouts—A1-FM maintained a success rate of 75.3%. This indicates that the model has learned robust features that transfer well to unseen environments.

![Efficiency and Exit Visualization](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05672v2/x6.png)
*Figure 5: Visualization of early-exit behavior. The numbers in the top-right corner indicate the layer at which the model exited, showing deeper evaluation for complex maneuvers.*

## Open-Source Transparency

Beyond its technical innovations, A1 distinguishes itself through a commitment to full transparency. The authors have released the entire training stack, including the code for data processing, the model weights, and the evaluation scripts. This open-source approach is intended to help the robotics community reproduce results and build upon the A1 framework, facilitating the development of even more efficient and capable robotic agents.

## Conclusion

The A1 framework represents a significant step toward practical, real-time robotic intelligence. By recognizing and exploiting redundancies in both visual processing and action generation, A1 demonstrates that large-scale VLA models can be made efficient enough for deployment on standard hardware. Its combination of adaptive inference, truncated flow matching, and extensive cross-platform training provides a blueprint for the next generation of efficient robotic controllers. As the field moves toward more complex, mobile, and multi-arm manipulation, the principles of budget-aware computation introduced by A1 will likely become increasingly essential.

keyInsights: Flow-matching trajectories often converge to correct action modes within few denoising steps (fewer than three), indicating redundancy in extensive iterative denoising.
Robot actions often evolve smoothly across consecutive control steps, suggesting that full VLA re-evaluation at every timestep may be inefficient.
Intermediate hidden states within the VLM backbone can encode sufficient spatial and visual features to adequately seed downstream action prediction, allowing for earlier exits.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models suffer from high computational cost and latency, primarily due to multi-billion-parameter VLM backbones and numerous iterative denoising steps in their action heads.
The computational demands of current VLA models restrict real-time deployment on commodity hardware, necessitating expensive hardware and significant energy budgets.
While VLM latency reduction has been explored, the action head often remains an unaddressed bottleneck, increasing overall inference time and hindering practical adoption.
results: A1 achieved an average success rate of 96.6% on the LIBERO benchmark and 56.7% on real-world tasks across four robot platforms (Franka, AgiBot, WuJie-Arm, Dobot-Arm), outperforming baselines like π0 and π0.5.

Inter-Layer Truncated Flow Matching reduced per-episode inference time by 72.3% (from 37.8s to 10.5s) while maintaining a 96.4% success rate, by reducing denoising steps from 10 to 2 and using warm-start initialization.
The model demonstrated robust zero-shot generalization on the LIBERO-Plus benchmark, achieving a 75.3% success rate even under significant distribution shifts, exceeding OpenVLA-OFT (69.6%).
solution: A1 integrates a Molmo-7B VLM backbone with a Qwen3-based flow-matching (A1-FM) or MLP (A1-MLP) action head, pre-trained on a diverse, large-scale robotic trajectory corpus.
A budget-aware adaptive inference mechanism jointly accelerates the VLM and action head using multi-exit training and early-termination based on action-consistency thresholding.
Inter-Layer Truncated Flow Matching warm-starts denoising for the FM head across VLM layers, significantly reducing iterative denoising steps from 10 to 2 and decreasing per-episode inference time.
summary.summary: Researchers from SYSU and MBZUAI developed A1, a fully open-source Vision-Language-Action (VLA) framework designed for efficient, real-time robotic control. A1 adaptively accelerates both its VLM backbone and flow-matching action head, achieving up to a 72.3% reduction in inference time while maintaining high manipulation success rates across diverse real-world and simulated tasks.

title: A1: A Fully Transparent Open-Source, Adaptive and Efficient Truncated Vision-Language-Action Model
